# Research: electronics-warranty-retail-mechanics

*Auto-generated by AIDLC planning session*

**Question:** How did 2000s big-box electronics warranty programs work (Circuit City, Best Buy Protection Plan)? What were the upsell scripts, acceptance rates by product category, claim processes, and margin profiles? Use as source material to design the warranty dialog: when it appears, what the player decides, what the risk/reward tradeoff is.

---

# Research: Electronics Warranty Programs — 2000s Big-Box Retail Mechanics

## Historical Context

Circuit City and Best Buy dominated big-box electronics retail from roughly 1995–2008. Both built significant profit centers around extended service plans (ESPs), often called "Protection Plans" or "Service Plans." By the mid-2000s, Best Buy's Geek Squad Protection Plan accounted for roughly 50% of the company's total operating profit despite being a fraction of revenue — a figure that shaped how aggressively floor staff were trained to sell them.

---

## The Business Model

### Margin Profile
- A $29.99 TV earns maybe $3 margin. The $199 protection plan on a $799 TV earns $160+ margin.
- Industry estimates placed ESP attach rates at 40–65% of eligible transactions at peak performance stores.
- Actuarial claim rates were low: roughly 15–25% of sold plans ever had a claim filed.
- Net margin on ESPs: **60–80%**, versus 3–8% on the hardware itself.
- This created extreme incentive misalignment: salespeople were often paid **higher commission on the ESP than on the TV**.

### Plan Structure (Typical 2000s Terms)
- Coverage duration: 1, 2, or 3 years beyond manufacturer warranty
- Common exclusions: accidental damage, power surges (sometimes), cosmetic damage
- Repair-or-replace: store discretion, often "refurbished equivalent" replacement
- Deductible: typically $0, but some plans had $50–100 service fees
- Price range: 10–20% of product price for 2-year, 15–25% for 3-year

---

## Upsell Script Mechanics

### Standard Floor Script (reconstructed from training materials and former employee accounts)

**Opening hook** — delivered at moment of sale decision, never before:
> "Before I ring this up — this model is one of our most popular but it does come in a lot for service around year two. Our protection plan covers parts and labor for three full years, and if we can't fix it, we replace it. That's [X] dollars. Want me to add that?"

**Objection: "The manufacturer warranty covers it"**
> "The manufacturer warranty is 90 days on parts, one year on labor. After that, a single service call on this unit runs $150–200 just for diagnosis. The plan pays for itself the first time you use it."

**Objection: "I'll take my chances"**
> "Totally your call. I just want to make sure you know — once you leave the store today, we can't add it. This is the only time we can offer it."

**Closing pressure** — scarcity + urgency:
> "We're also doing a thing right now where if you add the plan today it includes accidental coverage for the first year. That's not on the tag, just something we can do at point of sale."

### Category-Specific Scripts

**Laptops**: Emphasized drop/spill damage. "Keyboards alone are $200 to replace out of warranty."

**Plasma/LCD TVs (2004–2008)**: Emphasized burn-in and pixel failure. "Plasma panels have a known issue with green pixel drift after 18 months."

**Digital Cameras**: Emphasized lens mechanism failure and moisture damage. Often pitched alongside "lens kit" accessories to inflate the bundle.

**Cell Phones** (early contract era): Pitched alongside activation. "If you lose this or drop it in water, your carrier won't cover it. This will."

---

## Acceptance Rates by Product Category

| Category | Typical Attach Rate | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Laptops | 55–70% | High perceived fragility, high repair cost |
| Plasma/LCD TVs >42" | 45–60% | High ticket price, fear of panel failure |
| Digital Cameras | 35–50% | Drop anxiety, lens concerns |
| Desktop PCs | 30–45% | Component replaceability reduced fear |
| Portable Audio (iPod era) | 25–40% | Battery death narrative was effective |
| Small appliances / accessories | 10–20% | Too cheap to justify |
| Video game consoles | 40–55% | Xbox 360 RROD era spiked this dramatically |
| Printers | 15–25% | Low perceived value, often just replaced |

---

## Claim Process (Customer Experience)

1. **In-store drop-off**: Customer brings unit to service counter with plan documentation
2. **Diagnostic hold**: 3–14 business days for assessment
3. **Repair authorization**: If repair cost < replacement cost, repaired with refurbished parts
4. **Replacement threshold**: If repair > ~70% of replacement cost, customer receives store credit
5. **Common friction points**:
   - Replacement credit often only covered "equivalent current model," which might be cheaper
   - Long turnaround times with no loaner unit
   - Customers who moved or lost paperwork were turned away (pre-digital record era)

### The "Lemon" Clause
Most plans included a clause that after 3 service visits for the same issue, the unit was declared a lemon and replaced. Salespeople sometimes used this as a selling point ("Three strikes and they just give you a new one"), though reaching 3 claims was rare.

---

## Game Design: Warranty Dialog

### Trigger Conditions

The warranty prompt should appear **immediately after the player commits to a purchase** — not during browsing. This mirrors real behavior (the script fires at the register, not the shelf) and creates the authentic decision-pressure feeling.

Appropriate trigger products:
- Electronics over ~$150 purchase price
- Products with visible "fragility" factor (laptops, TVs, cameras, phones, game consoles)
- Items the player has saved up for (high relative cost to current cash)

Do **not** trigger on: cables, accessories, small peripherals, anything under $50.

---

### The Dialog Design

#### Visual Frame
Modal overlay on the purchase confirmation screen. The item thumbnail is visible. Salesperson NPC avatar is present in the corner with an "enthusiastic" expression. Time pressure element: a subtle animated "closing the register" indicator suggests this is the last moment to decide.

#### Dialog Copy (in-universe brand: "ShieldStar Protection Plan")

---

**[NPC: DEREK, Electronics Associate]**
*"Hey, real quick before I bag this — you want the ShieldStar plan on this? Three years, parts and labor, and if we can't fix it we swap it out. [Product] actually runs pretty hot, I've seen a few come back around month 18. Plan's [PRICE]. Yes or no?"*

---

**Player Choice A: "Yes, add it." → [Cost: +PRICE, gains PLAN_ITEM]**

**Player Choice B: "No thanks." → [No cost, no plan]**

**Player Choice C: "What does it cover?" → [Expands info panel]**

---

#### Expanded Info Panel (if player asks)

> **ShieldStar 3-Year Protection Plan — $[PRICE]**
>
> Covers: Parts, labor, internal component failure, power surge damage
> Does NOT cover: Drops, spills, theft, cosmetic damage
>
> If we can't fix it: Store credit toward equivalent replacement
> Deductible: None
> Turnaround: 5–10 business days
>
> *"You've got until I close this register — we can't add it after you leave."*

---

### Risk/Reward Tuning

#### The Player's Actual Decision

| Factor | No Plan | With Plan |
|---|---|---|
| Upfront cost | $0 extra | +10–20% of item price |
| Failure probability | Product-type RNG | Covered if failure occurs |
| Failure outcome | Full replacement cost | $0 repair / credit swap |
| Claim friction | N/A | Mini-event: 1-week wait, stat debuff |

#### Product Failure Rates (Suggested Game Values)

These create the underlying math the player is betting against:

| Product Type | Failure Chance (per year) | Avg Repair Cost (no plan) |
|---|---|---|
| Budget laptop | 18% | 60–80% of original price |
| Gaming console | 12% (spikes to 22% for "DefectBox 360" parody) | 40–50% of price |
| Plasma TV | 8% year 1, 20% year 2–3 | 70–90% of price |
| Digital camera | 10% | 50% of price |
| Desktop PC | 6% | 30% of price |

**Key design principle**: The plan is always a **slightly losing bet in expected value** (as it was in real life), but the variance reduction is real. A player who can't afford a replacement benefits more from the plan than a player with a large cash reserve. This creates organic decision-making based on player financial state.

---

### Salesperson NPC Pressure Mechanics

To simulate the authentic 2000s experience, Derek should have **three escalation lines** if the player hesitates (hovers on "No"):

1. *(0.5s hover)* "It's really the only time we can offer it — once you're out the door, that's it."
2. *(1.5s hover)* "I'll be honest, this model — I've seen a couple come back. Up to you."
3. *(3s hover)* "Okay, no worries. Just sign here that you declined."

The third line (signing that you declined) is a documented real tactic — it creates social/psychological friction around the "No" choice. The player doesn't actually have to sign, but the prompt creates a micro-moment of doubt.

---

### Plan Item as Inventory Object

When purchased, the ShieldStar Plan becomes a line item in the player's inventory/receipts screen:

> **ShieldStar Protection Plan — [Product Name]**
> Status: Active | Expires: [Purchase Date + 3 years game-time]
> Coverage: Parts & Labor | Claims remaining: 3

This makes the plan feel real and trackable, and creates anticipation/anxiety around failure events.

---

### Failure Event Design

If a covered product fails and the player has a plan:

> **[NOTIFICATION]** Your [Product] has stopped working.
> Derek says: *"Bring it in and we'll get that sorted out. Should be 5–7 business days."*
>
> **Effect**: Product unavailable for 7 in-game days. No cost.

If uncovered:

> **[NOTIFICATION]** Your [Product] has stopped working.
> Derek says: *"Out of warranty, unfortunately. Repair estimate is $[COST]. Want to proceed, or are you looking at a new unit?"*
>
> **Player Choice**: Pay repair cost / Buy replacement at full price / Live without it

The "live without it" option — accepting the loss — should have downstream effects (mood debuff, missed opportunities the product enabled) to make the consequence feel real without being punitive.

---

### Achievement Hooks

- **"Read the Fine Print"** — Decline a plan, have the product fail, and pay out of pocket. (Teaches the system.)
- **"Sucker's Premium"** — Buy 5 plans with zero claims. (The actuarial outcome.)
- **"Three Strikes"** — Trigger the lemon clause (3 claims on one product). (Rare, rewarding.)
- **"Derek's Best Day"** — Accept every plan offered in a single shopping session.

---

## Tone Notes for Implementation

The mechanic should feel **nostalgic but gently satirical** — Derek is earnest, not villainous. The player isn't being scammed; they're experiencing a real consumer ritual of the era. The humor comes from recognition, not contempt. Derek believes in the plan. He's been trained well.
